edge: ViTs have demonstrated the state-of-the-art performance in various vision-related tasks.
Adversaries are motivated to perform backdoor attacks on ViTs due to their success.
Vulnerability of traditional CNNs to backdoor attacks is well-known.
ViTs are compared to CNNs, with CNNs capturing pixel-wise local features by convolutions.
ViTs extract global context information through patches and attentions unlike CNNs.
Naively transplanting CNN-specific backdoor attacks to ViTs yields only a low clean data accuracy and a low attack success rate.
In this paper, a stealth and practical ViT-specific backdoor attack called TrojViT is proposed.
TrojViT generates a patch-wise trigger unlike area-wise trigger used by CNN-specific backdoor attacks.
Once the attacker inserts the Trojan into the ViT model, it can still produce normal inference accuracy with benign inputs.
When the attacker embeds a trigger into an input, the ViT model is forced to classify the input to a predefined target class.
Flipping only few vulnerable bits identified by TrojViT on a ViT model can be used to exploit the model.
TrojViT identified vulnerable bits on a ViT model and transformed it into a backdoored one
TrojViT can classify 99.64% of test images to a target class by flipping 345 bits on a ViT for ImageNet
ViTs have demonstrated higher accuracy than conventional CNNs in vision-related tasks

The ViT model can be backdoored and inserted with a Trojan using the well-known RowHammer method [18].
Unlike prior ViT-specific backdoor attacks [6, 17, 22] directly using an area-wise trigger, we propose a patch-wise trigger for TrojViT.
We propose a patch-wise trigger for TrojViT to effectively highlight the patch-wise information that the attacker wants the backdoored ViT model to pay more attention to.
During the generation of a patch-wise trigger, we present an Attention-Target loss for TrojViT to consider both attention scores and the predefined target class.
We create a tuned parameter distillation technique to reduce the modified bit number of the ViT parameters during the Trojan insertion.
Our TrojViT backdoor attack is more practical as we perform extensive experiments on various ViT architectures with multiple datasets.

Backdoor attacks focusing on CNNs adopt an area-wise trigger.
ViT-specific backdoor attacks using an area-wise trigger result in a low attack success rate and a low clean data accuracy.
CNNs capture pixel-wise local information via convolutions.
A ViT spatially divides an image into small patches, and extracts patch-wise information by attention.
CNNs extract local information, so the backdoor attacks on CNNs are not sensitive to the position of triggers.
In a CNN-specific backdoor attack, trig.1 and trig.2 should obtain very similar ASR. However, they produce a huge ASR difference in the backdoor attack on a ViT.

Prior area-wise trigger generation algorithms do not consider trigger position, and thus cannot distinguish trig.1 and trig.2. In contrast, if we can embed a small piece of the trigger into each critical patch of the input image by a patch-wise trigger, this patch-wise trigger can receive more attention from the victim ViT.
Potentially, a patch-wise trigger requires a smaller area than an area-wise trigger to achieve the same ASR, thereby greatly improving the stealthiness of a ViT-specific backdoor attack.
TrojViT is designed to attack ViTs, while TBT and Proflip use area-wise triggers and are dedicated to attacking CNNs.
Recent ViT-specific backdoor attacks such as DBIA, BAVT, and DBAVT still depend on an area-wise trigger that cannot absorb more attention to critical patches.
Although DBIA forces the victim ViT model to only put more attention to its area-wise trigger.
DBIA forces the victim ViT model to put more attention to its trigger during trigger generation.
ViT model aims to improve ASR for a predefined target class.
TrojViT tries to absorb more attention of the victim ViT model to its patch-wise trigger and maximize ASR for a predefined target class.
TrojViT has smaller trigger size in an input image than other works, providing better stealthiness.

Trojan Insertion of TrojViT algorithm involves creating trojan weights with minimized parameters based on the target weight and index ID. This relates to the concept of Attention-Target Loss as a component of the optimization process.

The Attention-Target Loss mentioned in the context considers both attention loss and target cross-entropy loss to optimize P. This shows a direct relationship between Attention-Target Loss and attention loss as components in the example.

The combination of attention and target losses in the Attention-Target Loss exceeds the performance of previous methods. This establishes a clear connection between the Attention-Target Loss and the target cross-entropy loss within the context.

The Attention-Target Loss aims to optimize P by considering attention weights. In the example provided, attention weights are calculated by performing softmax on the dot product of one query and all keys, emphasizing the relationship between Attention-Target Loss and attention weights.
Attention of patch i to patch j can be indicated using the attention loss function LlAT T N. This relationship is crucial in minimizing attention loss and optimizing to attack the target class.

Attention-Target Loss makes ˆX3 gain more attention and enlarges Sj by minimizing the attention loss function LlAT T N. This relationship is essential for optimizing the attack to the target class yk.
Attention-Target Loss adopts gradient surgery to avoid gradient conflicts between objectives. This relationship is key in maintaining consistency during training.

TrojViT modifies only the important parameters of the ViT model using a few test images with patchwise triggers. This relationship emphasizes the selective modification of crucial parameters for the attack.
TrojViT uses test images, even randomly sampled, for inserting the Trojan into the victim ViT model. This relationship showcases the approach of using test images without the need for training data.

Different tuned parameter thresholds (e) are set during Trojan insertion in the ViT model to skip non-critical model parameters. The choice of e impacts the ASR and the number of modified bits in the model.

DBAVT is a defense method designed for defending attacks on ViTs, but it may not work well for TrojViT as it detects Trojans based on patch processing sensitivity, which may not be effective for TrojViT.
TrojViT inserts a Trojan during inference, and thus is immune to such a defense.
Naively applying the defense technique of BAVT to TrojViT attacks greatly degrades the CDA by > 5% on various ViT models with ImageNet.

BAVT reduces the negative impact of an area-wise trigger by attaching a black patch to the position with the highest heat-map score.
BAVT reduces the negative impact of an area-wise trigger by attaching a black patch to the position with the highest heat-map score.
TrojViT creates a trigger composed of multiple pieces, each of which is embedded to one patch, through our Attention-Target Loss.
Therefore, replacing the patch with the highest attention score cannot prevent a patch-wise trigger of TrojViT.
Trojan insertion of TrojViT in Algorithm 1 heavily depends on the initialization of critical target Trojan parameters WT.
WT can be initialized as the weights of the last classification head layer.
The goal of our defense is to prevent an attacker from modifying the critical parameter matrix.
Instead of the original critical parameter matrix, we store the decomposed critical parameter matrix.
The decomposed matrices are stored in DRAM, which increases the attacker's effort to modify parameters while achieving a lower ASR.
The overhead of the defense method can be adjusted by different decomposition methods and the number of decomposed matrices.
The critical parameter matrix of the last classification head layer is decomposed with the decomposition technique proposed in [33].
The TrojViT performance is compared with and without the defense technique on the ImageNet dataset in Table 11.
The defense technique significantly reduces the ASR by over 21%.
The defense technique increases the attack overhead TPN by 2×.

We also show a potential technique to reduce the ASR of our TrojViT and increase the attack overhead.
